coredata.dc:title: Preparation of multicolor carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield and application in white LED
coredata.dc:creator: Da, Xianniu
Han, Zhaoxia
Yang, Zhijin
Zhang, Dawei
Hong, Ruijin
Tao, Chunxian
Lin, Hui
Huang, Yuanshen
coredata.dc:description: Using tartaric acid and triammonium citrate as environmentally friendly precursor reactants, multicolor fluorescent carbon dots with adjustable emission wavelength and high quantum yield were successfully synthesized via one-step solvothermal method by only changing the reaction solvent. The morphology, structure and optical properties of the carbon dots were investigated in detail. Characterization results showed that the as-synthesized carbon dots have good dispersibility, rich surface functional groups and high quantum yield (all over 40%). A high-quality white LED with CIE color coordinate (0.34, 0.35) was successfully fabricated using the prepared multicolor carbon dots as the light conversion material. The correlated color temperature was 5336 K, and the color rendering index was up to 83.1. The prepared LED emitted warm white light and was suitable for indoor lighting. It is of great significance to prepare multicolor carbon dots with high quantum yield by single-factor control, and the prepared multicolor carbon dots have potential and broad application prospects in the field of white LED.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Multicolor carbon dots
High quantum yield
White LED

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of multicolor CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield
Applications of the CDs in LED

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of the preparation for multicolor CDs.
(a) Photos of the as-prepared multicolor CDs under daylight (upper) and UV light (bottom); (b) Normalized PL emission spectra of the as-prepared multicolor CDs under optimized excitation.

TEM and HRTEM images of (a) blue CDs, (b) green CDs, (c) red CDs; Particle size distribution diagrams of (d) blue CDs, (e) green CDs, (f) red CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) XRD patterns (b) Raman spectra and the I(G)/I(D) ratios of the blue CDs, green CDs and red CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectra of the blue CDs, green CDs and red CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The C1s, N1s, O1s high-resolution XPS spectra of the (a) blue CDs, (b) green CDs and (c) red CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra (b-d) Excitation and emission spectra of the blue, green, red CDs solution. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of the blue, green and red CDs (the inset is the double exponential function fitting curve). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Schematic diagram of the structure of white LED; (b) Emission spectra of the white LED under different voltages; (c) Photos of the monochrome LEDs and white LED; (d) The CIE color coordinates of the monochrome LEDs and white LED.
The experimental parameters for the synthesis of multicolor CDs.
Percentages of different bonds according to the fitting results of the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s.

head.ce:title: Preparation of multicolor carbon dots with high fluorescence quantum yield and application in white LED

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Key Lab of Modern Optical System, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Key Lab of Modern Optical System
University of Shanghai for Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Key Lab of Modern Optical System, University of Shanghai for Science and Technology, Shanghai 200093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center of Optical Instrument and System, Ministry of Education, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Engineering Research Center of Optical Instrument and System

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center of Optical Instrument and System, Ministry of Education, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using tartaric acid and triammonium citrate as environmentally friendly precursor reactants, multicolor fluorescent carbon dots with adjustable emission wavelength and high quantum yield were successfully synthesized via one-step solvothermal method by only changing the reaction solvent. The morphology, structure and optical properties of the carbon dots were investigated in detail. Characterization results showed that the as-synthesized carbon dots have good dispersibility, rich surface functional groups and high quantum yield (all over 40%). A high-quality white LED with CIE color coordinate (0.34, 0.35) was successfully fabricated using the prepared multicolor carbon dots as the light conversion material. The correlated color temperature was 5336 K, and the color rendering index was up to 83.1. The prepared LED emitted warm white light and was suitable for indoor lighting. It is of great significance to prepare multicolor carbon dots with high quantum yield by single-factor control, and the prepared multicolor carbon dots have potential and broad application prospects in the field of white LED.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the energy problem has been concerned in various fields, and the lighting field is no exception. As the fourth-generation lighting source, white light-emitting diode (LED) has the advantages of small size, low energy consumption, and long life, which can not only effectively save a lot of energy, but also be far superior to traditional incandescent lamps in terms of luminous performance and efficiency [1,2]. Photoluminescence [3,4] and electroluminescence [5–7] are two mechanisms of white LED emission. Considering the energy problem, the photoluminescence mechanism is a better design idea than the electroluminescence mechanism due to its relatively low cost. There are two main preparation ways for commercial white LEDs: coating RGB (red, green and blue) phosphors on the ultraviolet chip [8] or coating yellow/green phosphors on the blue chip [9,10].

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel carbon nanomaterial, have been extensively studied due to their excellent light stability, adjustable emission wavelength, low toxicity, [11] good biocompatibility[12] and low cost [13,14]. These excellent properties promote their applications in biological imaging [15,16], sensors [17–19], photocatalysis [20–22], optoelectronic devices [23,24] and anti-counterfeiting [25]. To prepare high-quality white LEDs, preparing red, green and blue photoluminescent materials with high quantum conversion efficiency is a crucial step [26]. However, as far as we know, the preparation of multicolor fluorescence CDs with long-wavelength emission and high quantum yield (QY) is still a challenge for researchers [27]. In recent years, many researchers have reported the preparation of multicolor CDs with long emission wavelengths [28–30], but most of them were produced with the participation of strong acids or strong oxidants, which greatly increased the risk of experiments. For example, Fan et al. [31] used 1,3-dihydroxy naphthalene as the precursor and potassium periodate as the oxidant to synthesize red CDs with QY of 53% using a one-step solvothermal method. Lu et al. [32] synthesized the red CDs with QY of 31.82% using 1,3,5-benzenetrithiol and sulfuric acid using a solvothermal method at 200℃. Although they have successfully prepared red CDs with higher QY, it is not easy to prepare CDs with other emission wavelengths under this system. Using citric acid and urea as raw materials, Sun et al. synthesized blue to red CDs at different temperatures and for different time by solvothermal method [33], but the QY was low. Lu et al. [27] used o-phenylenediamine and citric acid as raw materials and used a strong acid to change the pH value of the aqueous solution, synthesized multicolor emitting CDs by one-step hydrothermal method. Although they have prepared CDs with different emission wavelengths under the same system, the QY of the prepared RGB CDs is not very high, especially the QY of long-wavelength emitting CDs.

In this paper, using tartaric acid and triammonium citrate as precursors, we successfully prepared the blue, green and red CDs with high fluorescence quantum yields (all over 40%) by the one-step solvothermal method at 180 °C for 8 h, the reaction solvent was water, ethanol and N, N-dimethylformamide, respectively. Tartaric acid exists widely in nature, and it is very convenient to extract. Therefore, tartaric acid is very economical and convenient as a carbon source. Triammonium citrate is rich in a large number of amino groups, which can increase the coupling of nitrogen to the prepared CDs. Through further experimental research, we found that the CDs of almost all colors can be successfully prepared just by adjusting the three solvents’ mixing ratios. The degree of carbonization and N content of the as-synthesized CDs were different in different reaction solvents. Therefore, the CDs prepared in different reaction solvents can emit fluorescence of different wavelengths. It is of great significance to prepare multicolor CDs with high quantum yield by single-factor control. A high-quality white light-emitting diode (LED) with Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinate (0.34, 0.35) was successfully fabricated using the prepared RGB CDs at a mass ratio of 2:1:1, the correlated color temperature (CCT) was 5336 K, and the color rendering index (CRI) was up to 83.1. The prepared LED emitted warm white light and was suitable for indoor lighting. The results indicated that the prepared multicolor carbon dots have potential and broad application prospects in the field of white LED.

Tartaric acid (TA, AR, ≥99.5%), Triammonium Citrate (TC, AR, ≥99.5%), N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF, AR,≥99.5%), Ethanol (C2H6O, AR, 99.7%), Methanol (AR, ≥99.5%) and Silane Coupling Agent (KH-792, 97%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All reagents and materials were used without further purification.

TA and TC were used as reagents, the solvent was water, ethanol and DMF respectively. In a typical experimental procedure for blue CDs, 0.75 g TA and 1.2 g TC were dissolved in water and sonicated them for 10 min to obtain a uniform solution. Then the solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. After the reaction was completed, it cooled down to room temperature and was taken out. The solution color changed from colorless to dark brown. Under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the solution color was blue. Similarly, green and red CDs can be prepared in the same way as blue CDs, except that the solvent was changed, using ethanol as the solvent for the green CDs and DMF as the solvent for the red CDs. The schematic diagram of the multicolor CDs preparation is shown in Fig. 1 . The obtained solution was further purified by column chromatography, and the mixed solution of methanol and water in the ratio of 3 to 1 was used as eluent. The purified solution was placed in a vacuum drying oven for drying. The obtained CD powder was re-dispersed in ethanol for storage.

In a typical experimental procedure, the prepared red, green and blue CDs were mixed in a ratio of 2:1:1, then dissolved in 3 ml KH-792 silane coupling agent (volume ratio 1:1) and sonicated for 10 min. The mixed solution was dropped on the surface of the 365 nm UV chip, then the UV chip was put in a drying oven and dried at 80 °C for 3 h.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM) image and high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) image of CDs were taken by JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope. The CDs powder was dissolved in ethanol and dispersed by ultrasonic vibration, and then dropped it on the ultra-thin carbon film for characterization. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CDs were recorded with a Rigaku MiniFlex 600 diffractometer in the 2θ range from 10° to 80° at a scanning speed of 5°/min, and the samples were spread on a quartz plate for XRD analysis. The Fourier infrared (FTIR) spectrum of the CDs was measured by Thermo Fisher Scientific IS5, and the CDs were placed on a KBr slide. The X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was measured by Thermo Fisher Scientific K-Alpha. The ultraviolet–visible absorption spectrum was measured with a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 1050 spectrophotometer. Horiba Dual UV-NIR fluorescence spectrometer was used to analyze the excitation and emission spectrum of CDs solution, and xenon lamp was used as the light source. The fluorescence decay curve of CDs was measured by Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 steady-state/transient response fluorescence spectrometer, and its fluorescence lifetime and yield were calculated. Everfine ATA-500 photoelectric analysis measurement system was used to analyze the spectrum distribution, CIE color coordinates, color rendering index (CRI) and correlated color temperature (CCT) of the prepared LED.

An integrating sphere in the Edinburgh Instruments FLS1000 steady-state/transient response fluorescence spectrometer was used to measure the absolute fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of the prepared CDs. By definition, the absolute QY, η, is the ratio of the number of photons emitted to the number of photons absorbed. There are two ways to measure QY with this instrument, and we used “Direct Excitation” method. With the “Direct Excitation” method, the absolute QY is calculated as follows:
(1)
η =
E B
-
E A
S A
-
S B

Where η is the absolute fluorescence QY of the test sample, S represents the spectral scan of the excitation scattering region of the sample and solvent, E represents the spectral scan of the emission region of the sample and solvent. A represents solvent and B represents sample. Note that the quantities
S A
,
S B
,
E A
and
E B
refer to the integral of the scans.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of multicolor CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para: Using TA and TC as reagents, multicolor CDs with high fluorescence quantum yield were successfully synthesized by solvothermal method in different solvents (water, ethanol, DMF and their mixed solvent), the experimental parameters for the synthesis of multicolor CDs are shown in Table 1 . We synthesized blue CDs in water, green CDs in ethanol, and red CDs in DMF, the emission wavelengths are 440 nm, 521 nm, and 602 nm, respectively. The QYs of the as-synthesized blue, green and red CDs are 43.6%, 41.2% and 44.1%, which are all over 40%. In previous reports, the QY of CDs prepared in the same system cannot all exceed 40%. We have summarized some previous reports on the preparation of multicolor CDs, as listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information).

By mixing the solvents in different ratios and changing the polarity of the mixed solvent, we have synthesized full-color wavelengths CDs. Fig. 2
(a) shows the photos of the as-prepared CDs under daylight and UV light. The optimized ratios of the mixed solvents for blue-green, cyan, yellow-green, orange CDs are H2O: DMF = 2:1, H2O: DMF = 3:1, Ethanol: DMF = 4:1, Ethanol: DMF = 2:1, respectively, the emission wavelengths are 485 nm, 506 nm, 553 nm and 580 nm. The normalized PL emission spectra of the as-prepared multicolor CDs are shown in Fig. 2 (b), we can see that the emission spectra almost cover the entire visible light band.

The size and morphology of the CDs are observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 3
(a-c), which corresponds to blue CDs, green CDs and red CDs. It can be seen that the CDs are spherical and uniformly dispersed without obvious agglomeration. Fig. 3 (d-f) show the statistical graphs of the particle sizes of blue CDs, green CDs, and red CDs, the average sizes of the CDs are 2.259 nm, 2.444 nm, and 3.069 nm, respectively. The right insets in Fig. 3 (a-c) show the corresponding HRTEM images of the CDs, which clearly have lattice fringes. The fringes spacing (0.21 nm) corresponds to the (100) lattice distance of graphitic carbon [34]. Fig. 4
(a) shows the XRD patterns of the as-synthesized multicolor CDs, there are a broad peak at 21° and a sharp peak at 26.7°. The peak at 21° is the peak of sp3 C in the graphene plane, which increases the interlayer distance. The peak at 26.7° is the (002) characteristic peak of graphite. With the red shift of the emission wavelength of the CDs, the diffraction peak at 26.7° becomes more and more obvious, indicating that red-CDs have a higher degree of graphitization and a higher degree of crystallinity [33]. Fig. 4 (b) shows the Raman diagrams of the CDs. There are two peaks at 1340 cm-1 and 1580 cm-1[35], which correspond to the disordered D band and the G band of graphite carbon. The intensity ratio of I(G) /I(D) is 1.31, 1.41, and 1.78, respectively. The increase in the ratio indicates that the degree of sp2 hybridization gradually increases, and the conclusion is consistent with the XRD and HRTEM results.

In order to further analyze the surface state and internal structure of the CDs, FTIR was used to characterize CDs. Fig. 5 shows the FTIR spectra, the absorption peak at 3300–3470 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, the absorption peak at 1700 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C = O, the absorption peak at 1638 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of C = N, and the absorption peak at 1462 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of C = C, the bands at 1400 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of C-N. The absorption peak at 1194 cm−1 is attributed to the vibrations of C-C [32,36–38].

To investigate the chemical bonds, element composition and functional group properties on the surface of the CDs, the samples were examined by XPS. Figure S1 shows the XPS survey spectra, three typical peaks correspond to C1s (285 eV), N1s (399 eV) and O1s (532 eV), which indicates that the main components of the CDs are carbon (C), nitrogen (N) and oxygen (O), The proportion of each component was shown in Table S2. Fig. 6
(a-c) shows the C1s, N1s, O1s XPS high-resolution spectra of the blue CDs, green CDs and red CDs, respectively. The C1s spectrum is composed of three peaks based on the peak fitting. The peak at 284.6 eV is assigned to sp2 C (C-C/C = C), which indicates that there are a large number of layered graphite structures in the core of the CDs. The peak at 285.7 eV is assigned to sp3 C (C-O/C-N), and the peak at 288.2 eV is assigned to O-C = O, indicating that there are many carbon-containing and oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of the CDs.

We calculated the percentages of different bonds of the CDs according to the fitting results of the high resolution XPS spectra of C1s, the results were shown in Table 2 . We can see that the degree of sp2 hybridization in the as-synthesized CDs gradually increases with the increase of the solvent polarity, and the surrounding oxygen-containing functional groups decrease, indicating that the degree of carbonization gradually deepens [39,40]. The N1s spectrum is composed of two peaks based on the peak fitting, the peaks at 399.3 eV and 400.4 eV are assigned to amino N and pyrrolic N, which shows that the N element is successfully coupled to the CDs. In addition, it can be seen that the pyrrolic N content of the CDs synthesized in solvent with larger polarity is more than that of the CDs synthesized in solvent with less polarity [41,42]. The O1s spectrum is composed of two peaks based on the peak fitting, the peak at 531.8 eV is assigned to C = O, and the peak at 533.2 eV is assigned to C-O, indicating that there are hydroxyl and carboxyl groups around [43].

Fig. 7
(a) shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of the as-synthesized blue, green, and red CDs. The absorption in 230–280 nm is derived from the π-π* electronic transition of the C = C bond, the broad peak at 350 nm mainly comes from the n-π* transition of the C = O bond, the degree of carbonization of the red CDs is deeper than blue, so the surrounding C = O bonds are much less, which corresponds to the XPS spectra. In addition, the green CDs and red CDs show some peaks in the visible wavelength range of 440–600 nm, which may be attributed to the n-π* transition of the aromatic sp2 structure containing C = N/C = O bonds [44]. Fig. 7 (b-d) show the excitation spectra of the blue, green, red CDs at 440 nm, 521 nm, 601 nm, and the emission spectra under different wavelengths of excitation light. We can see that the as-synthesized blue and green CDs have obvious excitation wavelength dependence, while the emission peak of the red CDs does not shift significantly. The excitation dependence of the fluorescence spectrum of the blue and green CDs may originate from the influence of surface functional groups. However, with the increase of carbonization degree, the eigenstate emission of the conjugated carbon nucleus of the CDs gradually occupies the main position of the fluorescence emission center. Therefore, the emission peak of the red CDs does not shift significantly.

In order to further understand the photoluminescence, the fluorescence lifetime decay curves of the ethanol solutions of blue, green and red CDs were measured under the excitation of 375 nm using the multi-dimensional time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) method, as shown in Fig. 8 . The embedded figure is the double exponential fitting curve. The decay curve of photoluminescence is calculated by the following equation [9]:
(2)
Y
t
=
α 1
exp
-
τ
t 1
+
α 2
exp
-
τ
t 2
+ ⋯

The measured curve can be fitted to two exponentials, where
α 1
and
α 2
are the percentages of the attenuation life of
t 1
and
t 2
, respectively. The average life can be calculated by equation (3) [10]:
(3)
τ
av
=
α 1
τ 1
2
+
α 1
τ 2
2
α 1
τ 1
+
α 1
τ 2

The fluorescence mechanism of the CDs is closely related to its surface state and carbon nuclear state [45]. The two radiation lifetimes
t 1
and
t 2
can be attributed to the internal recombination of the carbon nuclear state and the surface state, respectively [46]. The fitting parameters in Table S3 show that the as-synthesized CDs have a double exponential decay, their average fluorescence lifetimes are 10.26 ns, 9.47 ns, and 7.07 ns, respectively. We can see that the lifetimes decrease with the red shift of the emission wavelength. In addition, we can see that the proportion of
τ 2
gradually increases with the red shift of the emission wavelength, which indicates that the area of the carbon nucleus has increased. Based on the above analysis, it is found that the photoluminescence of the as-synthesized CDs is mainly composed of carbon nuclei light and surface light. With the change of the reaction solvent, the carbonization degree of the precursor can be changed, which leads to the change in emission wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the CDs in LED
ce:section.ce:para:
Because the as-synthesized CDs have high quantum yield and multicolor fluorescence emission, they were combined with 365 nm UV chips to prepare multicolor and white LEDs. First, we mixed the blue, green or red CDs solution with KH-792 silane coupling agent, and let it stand for 5 h to form a uniform gel-like liquid. The prepared liquid was coated on a 365 nm UV chip to prepare the monochrome LED, then put them in an oven to dry for 3 h. For white light LED preparation, the prepared blue, green and red CDs liquids were mixed in a certain proportion, then coated on a 365 nm UV chip, and dried for 3 h. The white LED is mainly composed of UV chip, gold wires, electrodes and CDs layers, as shown in Fig. 9
(a). To test the stability of the white LED, the luminescence spectra of the white LED were tested under different voltage drive. From Fig. 9 (b), it can be seen that only the luminous intensity changes as the voltage changes, the emission spectrum shape does not change. The photos of the prepared monochrome LEDs and white LED are shown in Fig. 9 (c), and their corresponding color coordinates are (0.17, 0.15), (0.31, 0.53), (0.58, 0.4) and (0.34, 0.35), respectively. The corresponding positions of the CIE color coordinates in the color coordinate diagram are shown in Fig. 9 (d). The prepared white LED has CCT of 5336 K and CRI of 83.1 when the test voltage is 3.6 V, which belongs to the warm light and is suitable for indoor lighting.

Using the one-step solvothermal method, we successfully synthesized full-color CDs by only changing the reaction solvent using TA and TC as reagents. The fluorescence quantum yields of the as-synthesized blue, green, red CDs were all above 40%. The prepared CDs were spherical in shape, and their average sizes were all less than 5 nm. Under the irradiation of excitation light, the multicolor CDs can emit multicolor fluorescence, the emission spectra almost cover the entire visible light band. The carbonization and polymerization degree of the precursors are different in different reaction solvent. Therefore, we obtained the CDs with different emission wavelengths. It is also found that different carbon atoms and nitrogen doping content of the CDs affected their luminescence properties. The as-synthesized CDs were used for monochrome LEDs and white LED. The color coordinates of the prepared white LED were (0.34, 0.35), the CCT is 5336 K, and the CRI is 83.1. The results indicated that the prepared multicolor CDs have a good application prospect in the field of indoor lighting.

Xianniu Da: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhaoxia Han: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Zhijin Yang: Software. Dawei Zhang: Project administration, Validation. Ruijin Hong: Supervision. Chunxian Tao: Investigation. Hui Lin: Formal analysis. Yuanshen Huang: Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was sponsored by the Shanghai Foundation for Science and Technology Innovation Action Plan (17142202700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (61775140, 61775141), Shanghai Pujiang Program (18PJ1408800) and Horizontal Cooperation Project of Teaching Research Office of Shanghai Municipal Education Commission (H-2020-302-121).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2022.139497.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
